entry.title: Arduino 1
entry.content:
Yesterday, I have designed an interesting ‘game’ that shows how many times you can press the button in 20 seconds.

(Image from Arduino)

To achieve this, I have used Arduino IDE(intergreted development environment) and Python as two main tools. First of all, the ‘pyserial’ needs to be installed, which is used to provide an interface to read information from the serial port . The ‘pyserial’ links the Arduino with Python.

Then, ‘serial’ is imported in Python.

Serial port is given in the Tools –> Serial Port, and this tells the IDE which type of socket the Arduino is connected to.

The baud rate is the rate at which information is transferred in a communication channel. Timeout is set because otherwise, the program will wait forever if it does not recieve the signal.

Then, there are some hardwares needed:

  1. A button
  2. A resistor
  3. Several conducting lines
  4. Breadboard
  5. Arduino board

The Schematic:

Here, the pin 7 is used to receive and send the signal, and the button is set to give the input signal. If the button is pressed, the digitalRead(pushButton) will give 0, and if the button is not pressed, digitalRead(pushButton) will give 1. When the button is first pressed, the ‘if’ syntax will not be run, and beforstate is given value 0. When the button is first bounced, the buttonState=1 and beforestate=0, so press time is added 1. This is basically how it works. When each time it is bounced up, the number is added one.

Then, Python is used to set 20 seconds and find the number of times.

‘time’ module is used here. time.time() is used to find the current time. Here, we round the time to the nearest second. .readline() is used to read one column of data. len(string)>0 is to ensure that not a blank is selected. .strip() is used to remove specific character, and the default is the space and the line break. encoding = “utf8” is used to change byte to string.

Finally, it looks like this:

I press the button 82 times in 20 seconds. A nice score, isn’t it?

Appendix:

  1. https://www.cnblogs.com/lanceyu/p/10201236.html
  2. https://chengnillcom.wordpress.com/2019/06/02/607/
  3. https://www.runoob.com/python3/python-simplestopwatch.html
  4. https://www.cnblogs.com/zqifa/p/python-7.html

entry.title: COVID-19 cases 2
entry.content:
The terrible COVID-19 cases are still spreading all over the world. Last week, I have analysed some cases in China, and today I decided to analyse some data abroad.

The data was from: https://www.kaggle.com/imdevskp/corona-virus-report

First of all, data is imported into the notebook by pandas. The data was updated utill May 21st.

I want to see the trend of confirmed cases and number of deaths in every country, so I decide to groupby the data in terms of ‘Date’ and ‘Country/Region’, the multiIndex.

However, the multiIndex is not convenient for the following processing, so I reset the index.

Then, I made a pivot table, so that I can draw a graph to visualise the trend in different country.

df1 is an array which contains the data of the chosen country. If I want to see cases in different countries, I just need to alter the country name in df1.

As shown in the graph, the rate of increase in confirmed cases in the US is tremendously higher than other countries. It seems to be an exponential growth. This is very strange, and I wonder why such a developed economy with so many high technologies suffered from this virus much more than anywhere around the world, so I search on the Internet. According to Jeffrey Sachs (2020)‘s opinion, which has been published on the CNN website, the failure of the US government is the main cause of this situation. In addition, the author suggested that everyone should wear a mask in public to avoid the spread of the disease. Also, people with contacting symptoms should be quickly isolated, so that other people will have less chance to be infected. In my opinion, the US is an international country. There are many people from all over the world maybe travelled to the US before they have been tested positive, and at the first stage, maybe many people did not realise how terrible this pandemic was, so they did not wear a mask. This makes more and more people infected. I think isolating the super-spreaders is very important, because 20% of infected patients may drive 80% of transmissions.

<Image from https://images.theconversation.com/files/312353/original/file-20200128-120039-bogv2t.png?ixlib=rb-1.1.0&q=45&auto=format&w=754&fit=clip>

As shown in the graph above, the geometric growth of the disease is very fast. What is more, according to Ying Liu et al (2020), the The reproductive number R0 of COVID-19 is higher compared to SARS coronavirus. ‘R0 is an indication of the transmissibility of a virus, representing the average number of new infections generated by an infectious person in a totally naïve population. For R0 > 1, the number infected is likely to increase, and for R0 < 1, transmission is likely to die out. The basic reproduction number is a central concept in infectious disease epidemiology, indicating the risk of an infectious agent with respect to epidemic spread.’

This is the deaths rate of this pandemic. As shown in the graph, the second largest deaths rate occured in the UK. I think this maybe due to the Herd Immunity or Community Immunity policy promoted by the UK governemnt. This means if many people in the area are infected, they will generate the anti-body by themselves, so they all become immuned to the diseaes. Consequently, those people who can produce the anti-body or have better immune system will survive, and will not be infected again due to stronger immune system, but those people who cannot generate the anti-body will die. This maybe the main reason for large number of deaths.

<Image from https://cdn.vox-cdn.com/thumbor/XrgJvLtsqbuzx02GwdVPbWfe-2g=/1400x1400/filters:format(png)/cdn.vox-cdn.com/assets/4196423/community_immunity.png>

Hopefully, people from all over the world can work together to fight against this pandemic. We will succeed in the near future!!

Reference:

  1. Ying Liu, Albert A Gayle, Annelies Wilder-Smith, Joacim Rocklöv, The reproductive number of COVID-19 is higher compared to SARS coronavirus, Journal of Travel Medicine, Volume 27, Issue 2, March 2020, taaa021, https://doi.org/10.1093/jtm/taaa021

  2. Sachs, J. (2020). Why the US has the world’s highest number of Covid-19 deaths. CNN.

entry.title: Dictionary 2
entry.content:
Today, I learn more about the ‘dictionary’ in Python.

Then, I have practised the following question.

Mean & Highest mark

This can be achieved using ‘Dictionary’ and some defined functions.

In lines 1 to 4, students’ names and their corresponding marks for 4 subjects are input into the dictionary. Here, the keys are names, because they are unique for small samples of people, and another dictionary is used for gathering marks with their corresponding subjects.

In lines 5-10, ‘mean’ function is defined by myself, which aims to find the mean score for particular subjects. Multiple assignment (多重赋值) is used as shown in line 7, which means that keys in ‘score’ will be assigned to ‘k’, and values in ‘score’ will be assigned to ‘v’, which is equivalent to k = score.keys() and v = score.values(). In addition, in line 8, ‘get’ method is used, so that when the input key has no corresponding values in the ‘score’, the return value will be 0. In doing so, one obstacle will not make the whole program crack down. Then, it is just summing and averaging.

In lines 12 -19, ‘highest’ function is defined, which aims to find the highest score. Here, I used the ‘append’ method instead of a = a + list(v.get(subject,0)) due to the fact that the results are integers which cannot be transferred to a list. Rather, ‘append’ here means that in the 1st line of ‘allStudents’, the specific subject(key), say ‘Chinese’, is being looked for. Then, the corresponding value, 88, is noted and the program will move to the next line. In the 2nd line, following the same process, 96, is noted, and so on. Furthermore, the next ‘for’ loop is used to find the person who has scored the highest mark. If the value of the ‘subject’(keys) is the maximum one, then the person(k) is noted.

Finally, as shown in figure below, the results are printed by calling those functions.

entry.title: CNN & Classification
entry.content:
Today, I have read and learnt some codes about the CNN (Convolutional Neural Network) used in classification problems.

The example that I read was to train a model and use it to recognise the hand-written numerals. The trained data and the test data are from the MNIST (Mixed National Institute of Standards and Technology database).

Other parts of the codes are similar to the previous one that I have read, but the most impressive one for me was the description of the CNN model.

Cov2D means 2 dimensional convolution. This is a process that a 5*5 matrix is used as a kernel and convolute with the pixel matrix. For example, the image, kernel, output and the output matrix are shown below. In doing so, some parts of the image are treated as a whole, so that the order of number of parameters needed to be trained decreased dramatically.

Although the number of multiplication before the activation function increases a bit, CNN algorithm has reduced a significant amount of training process. Take a 1D convolution as an example, the picture above shows a full connection network, while the picture below shows a 1D CNN. The number of parameters needed to be trained in the full connection network is 5 weights and 1 bias (6 in total). By contrast, the parameters needed to be trained in the 1D CNN is 3 weights and 1 bias (4 in total), which is less than that of the traditional network. Imaging that billions of data needs to be trained, the parameters to be trained is, say, 10000 times more using full connection network. In addition, for the traditional network, the multiplication before activation function is f(w1x1+w2x2+w3x3+w4x4+w5x5+b), which has 5 multiplication, while for the CNN, the multiplication before activation function is f(w1x1+w2x2+w3x3+b), f(w1x2+w2x3+w3x4+b), f(w1x3+w2x4+w3x5+b), which has 9 multiplication. The number of multiplication may affect the operating efficiency, but the number of parameter needed to be trained will affect the training efficiency.

What is more, the pooling layer is used here. The maximum pooling layer is to select a locally maximum pixel to represent this area. An example is shown below. This pooling layer will also reduce the calculation quantities, which increases the operating and training efficiency.

Finally, the Dense layers are used too, which are full connection layers.

In conclusion:

  1. CNN can reduce the number parameter needed to be trained
  2. Pooling layer can also reduce the number of calculation

​
​

entry.title: Dictionary
entry.content:
Recently, I learnt about some uses of ‘dictionary’ in Python.

Menu & Dictionary

Then, I wrote a programme to construct a menu. The code is shown below.

In the first 7 lines, the categories of snakes are printed, during the process of which the ‘center’ and ‘ljust’ methods are used to adjust the position of the text. In the following 6 lines, I construct a list called ‘menu’, which is used to include all kinds of snakes.__ A list is used due to the fact that there is an automatic mapping between the order and the corresponding snake, which makes it easier to call the element.__

Then, the time is obtained from system, followed by a ‘while’ loop.__ By the way, the ‘while’ loop is used, because it allows users to input as many times as they want. __Afterwards, ‘if’ conditions are used to decide which item the user chooses. In the last ‘if’, the ‘order’ is treated as a ‘dictionary’, the ‘keys’ of which is the order number, and the value of which is the quantity of the chosen snake. At the end of the ‘if’ condition, the quantity of the snake chosen is updated.

Finally, if the report is required to be printed, i.e. number 4 in the menu is chosen, the quantity is printed with corresponding snakes. If the user doesn’t want to have the report, i.e. number 5 is chosen, the interface will be closed. By the way, the first condition is to judge whether the number is 5, followed by 4, and then 1,2,3, otherwise, all the codes will be executed till 4 and 5, which makes the condition meaningless.

The result of the programme is shown below.

entry.title: Feelings after getting the award
entry.content:
Today, finally, I got the silver award for the 2019 BPho.

Looking at the certificate, I had many words to say. Although it is only a silver award, it is quite unique for me. I have known this competition since the summer holiday in 2019. Having a first look at the past paper of BPho, I nearly can’t do any of those questions, because I only knew a little from my IGCSE course, which was absolutely not enough. Then I decided to try my best to work out as many questions as possible. It was really a tough time for me, especially at the beginning. I went to school at daytime, so the only time for me was the evening. Every day, I felt so exhausted after a busy school day, but for the BPho, for my dream, I kept telling myself ‘Come on !’. A few weeks later, I managed to do some of the questions, and I felt a little proud for myself.

On 16th of November, 2019, I attended the BPho. After getting the paper, I focused on my paper and did not think about anything else. As time passed by, the questions became more difficult, and at some instant, I was a little bit impatient, because there were more and more questions that I can’t manage to do, but later on, I told myself ‘Calm down! Try your best, and it will be fine’. Therefore, one question successfully reminded me of a question about the spring oscillator, which I have done before, which inspired me a lot. I still remembered that question, which was about letting a double planks connected by two strings falling from a height, and the two strings will make the planks to do simple harmonic motion. This gave me a lot of confidence, and encouraged me to do more questions. Although it was difficult to pay 100% attention throughout the 160 minutes, and there were some questions I did wrong which I should have done it correctly, I was appreciated when the competition has finished, because I applied at least 70% of what I have learnt. Meanwhile, I felt a little bit upset, because I thought there would be some questions about the electric circuits in section 2, such as the Kirchhoff’s law and the Wheatstone bridge, but they did not appear in the paper.

Now, I got the result. I was fortunate, and I want to say thank you to my father, who helped me a lot during the preparation of the BPho, and of course, I want to say thank you to myself. It is me who kept challenging the extreme and never gave up.

This silver award is just a starting point. There is still a long way to go. Well done and keep it up!

entry.title: Image Processing
entry.content:
Recently, I have read some open-source codes about the Image Processing. The main modules used are ‘opencv’, ‘numpy’,’matplotlib’.

Then, the image is read into the file, and the type of which is np.ndarray. By the way, I have learnt that the image is stored in the computer in the form of matrix, where each pixel is consist of RGB 3 channels, and the value of each channel is between 0-255 as there are 8 bits in each channel. Therefore, through different combination of colours, we can see the colourful images. In addition, the shape of the image read is 506 * 675 * 3, meaning that the image is 506px in height, 675px in width and has 3 colour channels.

After this, if we want to see the image in the notebook, we can use plt.imshow() to show the image, and convert it from BGR to RGB colours. Also, we can show the grayscale image by converting BGR to GRAY.

BGR:

Grayscale:

After the pre-processing, the slicing can be used to choose the part you want.

Just like the slicing of list or string in Python, [start:stop] can be used to slice. Hence, the height and width can be chosen. Furthermore, yellow rectangles can be drawn to locate the chosen area. Note that the rectangle is drawn, and the parameter is the coordinate of the diagonal.

Additionally, binary image can also be shown, meaning that value greater than the threshold will be changed to 255, and that smaller than the threshold will be changed to 0, so that there are only two colours in the matrix, black and white, and 0 stands for black, 255 stands for white. The higher the threshold. the simpler the processed image, but there may be some loss in comparison with the original one. If the threshold is low, the processed image may be closer to the raw image, but there may be some noise in it.

What is more, the edge detection can be achieved using Laplace operator. By the way, I have learnt the simplest meaning of convolution. Just put matrix B on the matrix A, and multiply with the corresponding element. For example, if B is on the left upper corner of A, than the calculation should be 3 * 0 + 3 * 1 + 2 * 2 + 2 * 0 + 2 * 0 + 0 * 1 + 3 * 0 + 1 * 1 + 2 * 2 = 3 + 4 +1 +4 = 12, which is written in the left upper corner of matrix A * B.

In conclusion, I have learnt the following briefly:

  1. Images are stored as matrix.
  2. Different values in the matrix (0-255) represents the colour of R/G/B
  3. To process the image is actually to change the pixel represented by the matrix. It is an algebraic calculation
  4. 2D Convolution is calculated by multiplying corresponding elements
  5. Binary value can show the edge of an object
  6. Images have many types, such as RGB and Grayscale. Grayscale image is often used to process, and then convert it into the RGB or BGR form.

entry.title: Markdown语法
entry.content:
Hello!

  1. https://imgchr.com/

entry.title: List 2
entry.content:
Recently, I learnt some further knowledge about the list in python and do some more exercises.

Metro tickets

First of all, I wrote a code to help determine the price of the metro 1 tickets.
If the customer enter the starting station and the terminal station, this program can automatically work out the price of the tickets based on the price interval. The code is shown below:

The main point in this code is the__ ‘index’__ method, which can return the position of the element in the list, so that the number of stops can be calculated, and therefore it can be compared with the price interval.

Narcissistic number 水仙花数

The second code I wrote was about ‘Narcissistic number’ (水仙花数) , which means the 3-digit number equals to the sum of its own digits each raised to the power of the number of digits.

I think the main point here is to separate the 3 digits of the 3-digit number. Actually, this is more to do with the__ ‘for’ loop__.

Consumer preference & dot product of vector

The third code was to find the most relevant movie according to the customer’s preference. This can be done using the dot product of the vector.
Assuming that there are only 3 characters which can describe a movie, which are ‘comedies’, ‘romantics’, and ‘action’, and there are up to 5 marks indicating the level of each character. For instance, if the movie is full of comedy and nothing to do with romantic, a few action shoots, then the score should be 5,0,3. Hence, the customer can enter the level of preference in each character, and the program can help him/her find the best one between movie 1 and movie 2.

Find ‘c’ in DNA

The fourth one I did was to find the number of ‘C’ character in a given DNA.

I think the main point here is to separate each element of DNA, and then make a judgement.

Monte Carlo method & area under the curve

Finally, I wrote a code, using Monte Carlo method to find the shaded area D.
Monte Carlo method is basically to generate 1000 points randomly. The probability of the 1000 points landing on the shaded area is approximately the area of the shaded region, given that ‘l’ equals to 1 in the figure.

In the codes above, x and y are the coordinates generated randomly. The letter ‘m’ stands for the number of points landing on the shaded region, while the letter ‘n’ stands for the total number of points. This can be done due to the fact that the probability of points landing on region D equals the area of D divided by the area of the square, which is 1 in this case. Actually, the larger the sample size, the more accurate the results will be. Later on, I have known that the advantage of the Monte Carlo method is that when the calculus doesn’t work, it still can be applied.

entry.title: Linear Regression
entry.content:
Today, I have learnt how to do linear regression using ‘sklearn’ LinearRegression models.

<Image from https://us.toluna.com/dpolls_images/2019/02/25/a8e2102e-f7ff-4716-9c1d-4b5c2d1dfb22_x365.jpg>

First of all, some points are plotted, and the physical meanings of these points are the size of a pizza and the corresponding cost. As shown in the graph, it looks like a linear line, so Linear regression can be used to make a prediction of the cost of a pizza.

Here, the shape of np.array([12]) is (1,), which means it is only a 1D vector, so we need to convert it into a 2D matrix or array, so that it matches the input of the model. Hence, ‘.reshape(-1,1)’ converts the shape of np.array([12]) from (1,) to (1,1), and the predicted cost is $13.75 for a 12 inches pizza.

The green line is the predicted linear relationship between cost and diameter, and the blue points are the actual distribution of points. Although there is some loss between real value and predicted value, the overal trend is correct.

‘idx’ stands for the index of the X(0,1,2,3,4,5). ‘x’ stands for the value of X ([6],[8],[10],[14],[18],20). The blue points are [x,y[idx]], and the orange points are [x,yr[idx]], and these two points are connected by red lines.

Linear regression is very useful in our daily lives, and most of pattern can use linear regression to make predictions. Although there will be some loss, the linear relationship will overall correctly describe the trend.

entry.title: Feelings and Reflections on the Garbage Classification Programme
entry.content:
Being a good student requires a variety of skills including time management, teamwork, keeping good focus and facing challenges, which I have learnt during my experience of taking part in the Garbage Classification Programme which aims to determine which class the rubbish belongs to through image identification and CNN algorithm.

<Image from https://youth-time.eu/wp-content/uploads/2018/11/b7d41aa04e4da12ee42f355b9c8e1b10_XL.jpg>

At the beginning, I had absolutely no idea what the Garbage Classification was and how can it benefit people all over the world. During the process of learning, I gradually found the importance of Garbage Classification which can benefit our economy, society and ecosystem.

< Image from https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/1BFF/production/_109676170_gettyimages-1166817364-2.jpg >

First of all, with increasing amount of rubbish produced every day and deteriorating environment, people have increasing concern about how to deal with a significant amount of rubbish and the concept of garbage classification was presented. With classified rubbish, the soil erosion will be greatly reduced, because some of the rubbish, such as battery and plastic bags, are not biodegradable, meaning that they cannot or it takes very long time for them to be broken down by the soil. Staying in the soil, those rubbish will make the field useless and decrease the output for the agriculture goods, which will negatively affect the economy.

<Image from https://res.cloudinary.com/fleetnation/image/private/c_fit,w_1120/g_south,l_text:style_gothic2:%C2%A9%20Ashley%20Cooper,o_20,y_10/g_center,l_watermark4,o_25,y_50/v1495745317/sfjlaup4gwdorkymjnqz.jpg>

In addition, some rubbish, such as rot food, will emit gases with bad smell and attracts many flies, which makes citizens very uncomfortable when passing through the region. This has worsened the environment significantly. What is more, some animals will accidently take in some the toxic plastic and die in a few months, which makes the ecosystem unbalanced.

Therefore, the garbage classification is needed to improve the situation. Different classes of rubbish can be reused in different ways, so that the resources can be used more efficiently. For example, disposable chopsticks, which are widely used in China, can be reprocessed to produce paper which will be otherwise produced by cutting down thousands and millions of trees. Also, some rubbish, such as wooden stuff, can be used to burn to produce electricity and providing heat for city dwellers in winter. As a result, different classes of rubbish can be sent to different factories to reprocess them and then reuse them. Therefore, instead of just landfill unclassified rubbish, we can reuse 60%-70% of the rubbish according to their classes to change them from rubbish to resources, and in the battle between human beings and rubbish, the rubbish can be changed from enemies to friends.

<Image from https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0773/5915/articles/Ryclying_pic_blog_2_1600x.jpg?v=1455084942>

However, in China, a large proportion of people are only encouraged to do the garbage classification, but they do not know how. Many people cannot tell which class the rubbish belongs to, so although there are different rubbish bins, they cannot correctly throw the rubbish into the correct bin. Therefore, I decided to learn some AI techniques to give information to people about the classes of the rubbish and help them classify the rubbish more easily. Although some people might argue that it is impossible for the machine to improve the garbage classification, because different classes of rubbish was put together into the rubbish bag by each households and it is very hard for the machine to identify the rubbish in the bag and then classify them, I holds a different view. In some occasions, such as in the airport, people often throw only one or two rubbish into the rubbish bin instead of a bag of them. The AI machine can be used in these situations, telling people the class in which the rubbish should be thrown, so that people get more knowledge about the classes, and when they repeat this in many situations for many times, they will get used to this way of thinking. Therefore, they can correctly classify the rubbish at home.

<Image from https://i.ytimg.com/vi/7C6I_wuvvhY/maxresdefault.jpg>

In reality, in Shanghai, China, it is compulsory for local citizens to do garbage classification, and some people, especially the old people, have little idea of the classes, and this has caused some trouble for their normal daily life. Citizens need to spend a lot of time searching for the type of the rubbish, which is very inefficient and boring. This may lead to some complains among the society, and give disincentives for citizens to do the meaningful things. Therefore, I wonder whether this complex thing can be done easily and interestingly, so I came up with the idea of connecting the garbage classification with modern technology instead of just a rule. The CNN algorithm of machine learning can train a model for a machine which can be used to identify the rubbish quickly and accurately. For instance, citizens only needs to show the rubbish in front of the camera, and then the class will be shown on the displayed screen, so that it will arouse the curiosity of children, adults, mid-aged people and aged-people, so that people can do the meaningful things happily. Due to the fact that it is not necessary for each one to know exact class of the rubbish, the garbage classification campaign can be done more effectively and burden of memory on residents can be reduced.

<Image from https://encrypted-tbn0.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcS9dWakreHNMCnKp2e05FKeJJhqJ4IiGyz_eJr0YDQPSG8LLZyT&s>

In conclusion, after doing the programme, I have spent some valuable time thinking about the future prospects of the garbage classification that can be applied to our real life, and currently I am still training the model and improve the algorithms. Hopefully, one day in the future, the AI techniques of machine learning and image identification can be used widely in the world, and this is my final intention of doing this programme.

entry.title: My participation in 2019-nCoV Prevention & Treatment Project
entry.content:
Yesterday, on the 30th of January, the World Health Organization has declared that the coronavirus in China a global health emergency.

I was shocked when hearing this news early in the morning. Initially, I thought it was only a regional outbreak in Wuhan, the city at the centre of the outbreak, but then the virus has spread to almost everywhere in China, and now around the world. Until 30th of January, the number of deaths has increased to 170, and there were 7711 confirmed cases and 1370 suspected cases.

First of all, I felt so depressed about this news, and wanted to thank for all the doctors and nurses fighting for the coronavirus, and felt so sorry for those who have suffered from this virus during the Chinese New Year. Then, I decided to do something for patients and medical staffs from all over the world, especially Wuhan, China.
As a citizen, I have been told by the news that the full protecting clothes for medical staffs and facial masks everyone were in short supply but in high demand. In addition, I realised that some materials must be in shortage, because this size of this 2019-nCoV was unprecedented. Therefore, I wanted to use my faint action to help patients who needs the warmth from everyone. As the saying goes, ‘Actions speak louder than words’, I decided to donate my own money from the ‘red packets’ to help people suffering from this disease, especially in Wuhan.
Fortunately, I found the ‘Novel Coronavirus Prevention & Treatment Projects’ and donated 500 RMB to the Project Hope, a charity organisation. Although, the money itself has no intrinsic value, the compassion and kindness behind this activity is invaluable, which cannot be measured using money.
Furthermore, I wanted to say thank you to foreign people who hold great concern about the fighting for virus in China. It was your greeting and encouragement that gave us confidence and strength. The virus does not need a passport, and we are the community of shared future for mankind. Virus can be isolated, but the compassion and kindness cannot. The compassion and kindness are like a bridge, connecting people from all over the world.

Finally, I hope that with appropriate prevention and confidence, which can be seen as the best vaccine, we can definitely win the smokeless battle!

entry.title: Garbage Classification
entry.content:
(image from https://sce3a3b6c0d5pq-sb-qn.qiqiuyun.net/files/default/2019/07-16/103730af10be884644.jpg)

Recently, I took part in a competition about the garbage classification, which aims to classify the garbage into 198 categories by using image processing and CNN algorithms. The ultimate goal is to make the machine able to identify the garbage and tell the households which rubbish bin should this kind of rubbish go into. Hence, a lot of machine learning is needed to be done at first to make the machine familiar with all kinds of rubbish, and this is what the programme mainly about.

First of all, the data needs to be pre-processed. There are 80,000 images that are available to be trained. The original format of the file is JSON (JavaScript Object Notation), so the data is read into the code using json.load() method, and the return of which is a dictionary, and there are 6 keys in the dictionary, which are 'infor', 'licenses', 'images', 'type', 'annotations', 'categories' respectively.

In order to make it easier to find the name of the rubbish, names in the categories are given to a new list called names.

Then, we can randomly choose an image from the train set to have a look at it.

Note that as the index of the list names starts with 0, the index of the category_id needs to -1 to match the index.

As shown below, this is a randomly chosen umbrella.

After this, the id, label, name and filename of those rubbish can be rewritten into a new csv file called train.csv, and the process of pre-processing data is finished.

The train.csv looks like this:

Secondly, we need to train the garbage classifier. As usual, the modules are imported, but a new thing is introduced here, which are shown below. The first line aims to show the Chinese label normally, and the second line is used to show the -ve sign normally.

Then, pandas module is used to read the csv file which is created previously, and the format is DataFrame.

After reading the file, keras.preprocessing.image.ImageDataGenerator is used to load the data instead of using RAM to load the data. There are some pros and cons of each. If the data is loaded using RAM, the speed of which is very high as it directly access the memory in the computer, while if the data is loaded using keras.preprocessing.image.ImageDataGenerator, the memory will not be occupied too much. Hence, due to the fact that usually laptops do not have too much memory, generator is used here to load the data.

The first layer is the 2D convolutional network, and the size of the kernel is 5 * 5, meaning that a 5 * 5 matrix moves along each line and convolute with each element, so that the number of calculations is significantly reduced. The next layer is a 2D Max pooling one. meaning that the maximum number in a 2 * 2 matrix is chosen to represent the whole 2 * 2 matrix, so that the number of calculation will be reduced greatly although there may be a little bit loss of detail, and after which this process is repeated, followed by a flatten layer to flatten the multidimensional matrix to a 1D matrix. This is used as input to the full connection Dense layer. Hence, the summary of the model looks like this:

As shown in the table, there are 2,441,162 parameters needed to be trained, so it takes a long time to train the model.

Here, the .fit_generator() is used to train the model. steps_per_epoch = STEP_SIZE_TRAIN means that after the generator returns data for STEP_SIZE_TRAIN times, one epoch is finished. epochs means the times of iteration. After this, the model is evaluated and saved.

Then, we can use the saved model to make some predictions.

Note that the update() method inserts the specified items to the dictionary. It is like the append() method.

In the 8th line, np.expand_dims() changes the shape of the x (pixels of image) from a 122 * 122 * 3 matrix to a 1 * 122 * 3 matrix, so that it matches the format of the input of the model. Furthermore, in the 12th line, the pred has a shape of 1 * 198, because the last neuron has 198 output, and the value of which stands for the probability of being one of the 198 categories of the rubbish. Hence, the largest probability is found using argmax() method.

Finally, we can randomly choose 20 images to visualise the predicted result.

As shown in the graph, there are some incorrect prediction. For example, in the (2,3) image, it is like a ceramic, but the predicted result shows that it is a plastic cup. Therefore, some improvement of the model is needed to be done, and I will learn some improvement measures in the next week.

entry.title: List
entry.content:
A few days ago, l have learned the lists in Python.

Then, I practised 3 questions.

Change odd number to even number

First of all, for a given list which has only positive integers in it, if the element in it is an odd number, then add one so that it becomes an even number. For example, if the given list is [1,2,3,4,5,6], then the returned list should be [2,2,4,4,6,6]

Using a ‘for’ loop, each element can be judged whether it is an odd number. If it is, the original number is replaced by the new even number.

‘Commodity’ & ‘price’

Secondly, I wrote a program code to create a list by concatenating a given list which range goes from 1 to n. For example, if the given list is [‘p’,’q’] and n=5, then the output should be [‘p1’,’q1’,’p2’,’q2’,’p3’,’q3’,’p4’,’q4’,’p5’,’q5’]

Then, I realized that this program code has a practical use. If the an retailer needs n different commodities with corresponding price, the tags can be printed using this program code.

Unique elements

A program code which can accept two input lists and returns a new list which contains only the unique elements from both sides.

At the beginning, I have tried to define a function called ‘unique’ by myself. The code is posted below.

Initially, I made an empty list called ‘empty’, which is used for inputting unique elements of the lists. Then, elements of ‘lst’ is separated and put into the variable called ‘character’. The next step is to judge whether the empty list has already had the element that is picked from ‘lst’ using ‘not in’ method. If the element is repeated, then it does not satisfy the ‘if’ condition, so nothing will happen. If the element was not in the ‘empty’ list, then it will be appended to the ‘empty’ list. Finally, the elements in the ‘empty’ list is sorted in ascending magnitude using ‘sort’ method. When calling the ‘unique’ function, unique elements in both lists will be printed.

Afterwards, I accidentally found a built-in function ‘set’ which can achieve exactly the same thing as the defined function ‘unique’.

As shown above, the output for this is like [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]. This shows how powerful the method of __function ‘set’ __is ! It can find the unique elements in a list and rearrange them from the smallest to the biggest one.

entry.title: Matplotlib 2
entry.content:
Today, I have looked at some methods of matplotlib.

.pairplot()

‘.pairplot()’ is used to Plot pairwise relationships in a dataset.

First of all, the data is loaded. This data is about the sepal widths, sepal length, petal width and petal length of 3 different kinds of Iris Flower.

‘Iris’ is a ‘dataFrame’. “hue=’species’” means different colours are used to represent different species of Iris flower. ‘size=2.5’ means each diagram has the size of 2.5. As shown in the graph, many characteristics have shown linear relationship, such as petal length and petal width.

.FaceGrid()

This class maps a dataset onto multiple axes arrayed in a grid of rows and columns that correspond to levels of variables in the dataset. The plots it produces are often called “lattice”, “trellis”, or “small-multiple” graphics.

The data of tips picked is loaded.

First of all, a new column in the ‘tips’ ‘dataFrame’ is added, called ‘tip_pct’ which is calculated to represent the proportion of tips of the total bill paid. Then, ‘sex’ and ‘time’ columns are picked to represent the x axis and y axis respectively. bins=np.linspace(0,40,15) means the x axis starts from 0 and ends at 40, with 15 equal intervals. As shown in the graph, Male pays more bill at dinner. Maybe it is due to the fact that males like to treat others or invite others and have a party at dinner.

.factorplot()

Factor plot can be used to show the distribution of one parameter in another parameter, such as the distribution of total bill in each day.

‘day’ is the x axis and ‘total bill’ is the y axis. “kind=’box’” means the diagram is a box-whisker diagram which shows the upper quartile, the lower quartile , the mean, the highest value and the lowest value.

.jointplot()

This is used to Draw a plot of two variables with bivariate and univariate graphs

Here, “kind=’reg’” means regression, so the linear regression is automatically done. The univariate graphs are shown at the top and right of the graph, and the bivariate graph is shown at the centre. The shaded area represents the loss of the regression.

entry.title: Loops
entry.content:
Last week, I have learned 2 different types of loops in Python, which are__ ‘for’ loop__ and ‘while’ loop. I think the main difference between these 2 loops are that ‘for’ loops can deal with the situation where the number of loops is known, and ‘while’ loops are chosen if the number of loops is unknown.

Later, I did some practise. For example, I wrote some codes to display the pattern like this.

Pattern

After writing these codes, I realized that if you want to print in a line, just use comma to separate different items, or add (end =’’) .

Multiplication Table

In the following day, I wrote a program to show the multiplication table like this.

This is an example of a nested loop. The inside part will be executed first, and then the outside part. Adding (print(‘’)) in the last line is a very smart idea, because the end of each line will be occupied by a blank, so that the next figure begins in the next line.

entry.title: Matplotlib
entry.content:
Today, I have learnt some useful methods about the matplotlib.

Area and population in California City

<Image from https://ontheworldmap.com/usa/state/california/map-of-california-max.jpg>

First of all, the csv file is loaded by pd.read_csv().

The lattitude and longitude of the city and its corresponding area and population is the interested data, so they are selected from the file.

Scattered graph is used here. The x-axis stands for the longitude of the city and the y-axis stands for the lattitude of the city. Different colors are used to represent different number (density) of population. As the population is too large, the log scale is used to represent the large number. s is the size of the point on the scattered diagrame which represents the area of the city. cmap is the parameter used to set the type of colormap, such as ‘binary’ and ‘viridis’.

Here, the scale used in the x-axis and y-axis is the same. What is more, areas of 100, 300, 500 are used to give an approximation of the relation between the size of the circle and the area. Note that in line 11, x value and y value are none, so the legend is set using labels instead of x,y values, so plt.legend() must be used to show the legend. Otherwize, the legend will not be shown.

The graph shows the distribution of area and population from North-west to South-east in California.

2D Histogram

The Mulrivariate Gaussian Distribution is used to generate the x-axis and y-axis of the graph. bins parameter is used to define the number of equal-width bins in the range.

This diagrame has very important applications in my opinion. For example, in a shop, the enterprise can have an idea of the popularity of different goods by looking at the density of people in that area. This can be achieved by using an infrared detector which detects the heat emitted from human beings. The darker the color, the more people there are in that area. In doing so, the enterprise can adjust the supply of different products according to different demand to maximise their profit.

<Image from https://thumbs.dreamstime.com/z/people-group-infected-influenza-virus-under-thermal-imaging-camera-aka-thermal-unit-modern-airport-passenger-crowd-healthcare-171922450.jpg>

entry.title: Matplotlib 3
entry.content:
Today, I have learnt more methods of matplotlib by reading codes about the scores of Marathon.

<Image from https://www.badenmarathon.de/fileadmin/Badenmarathon/Bilder/Fotos/Start_Ziel/Start_2019>

It looks like this:

There are 4 columns which are age, gender, split, and final respectively, among which the ‘split’ column stands for the time taken for half distance of Marathon race, and the ‘final’ stands for the total time taken for the Marathon race.

However, the datatype of ‘split’ and ‘final’ are all ‘object’, meaning that the time is treated as a string which is not convenient for the following processing, so the ‘convert_time(s) ’function is defined, aiming to convert the datatype of ‘split’ and ‘final’ from ‘object’ to ‘timedelta’.

First of all, string before ‘:’ and after ‘:’ are splitted, so that the first string is the hour, the second is the minutes, and the third is the seconds. These values are given to ‘h’(hour), ‘m’(minute), ‘s’(second) respectively, so that a Timedelta variable is generated.

Then, the time in seconds of half distance and that of total distance is added to the original DataFrame by adding two more columns called ‘split_sec’ and ‘final_sec’.

Then, the time taken for half distance and total distance can be visualised using seaborn.

‘jointplot’ is used to draw a plot of two variables with bivariate and univariate graphs. Also, the dashed black line starts from (4000, 16000) to (8000, 32000), representing that the time taken for half distance is half of the total time taken, i.e. time taken for first half distance equals to that of the last half distance. This means that the athlete runs with constant velocity thought the whole Marathon. In addition, the blue hexagons represents the actual time taken. As shown in the graph, most people take longer time to run the last half distance, maybe due to the fact that they get tired after finishing the first half distance, so they cannot maintain that speed. Although this is the case, there are a few points that is below the dashed black line, meaning that some people use shorter time to finish the last half distance, i.e. they accelerates in the last half distance.

In order to see the proportion of people who run faster in the last half distance, a new column called ‘split_frac’ is added to the original DataFrame.

‘data[‘split_sec’] / data[‘final_sec’]’ stands for the proportion of time used in the first half distance. If the output is greater than 0.5, ‘2 * data[‘split_sec’] / data[‘final_sec’]’ will be greater than 1, so ‘1 - 2*data[‘split_sec’] / data[‘final_sec’]’ will be a negative value. This means that the last half distance takes up less proportion of time, i.e. they accelerates in the last half distance and vice versa.

A graph is plotted, which shows that only a small proportion of bars are less than 0, meaning that only a small proportion of people run faster in the last half distance. If we want to know the exact number of people who accelerates in the last half distance, the following code can be used to find it.

The output is 251. From this mask, we know that out of about 40,000 people, only 251 people run faster in the last half distance, and maybe they have a greater chance to win the Marathon race.

entry.title: Neural-Network-based Iris Flower Classification
entry.content:
Today, I have read and learnt some codes about neural network used in classification.

There are 3 types of Iris Flowers, which are Setosa, Versicolour and Virginica respectively. The code is used to determine the type by considering its petal length and petal width.

image from: https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/styles/primary_image_in_article/public/image_nodes/iris-flowers.jpg?itok=RvCxClOE

First of all, the sepal width, sepal length, petal length, petal width and iris class are read from a csv file, and 5 numpyarrays are used to store the data.

Then, in order to visualise the patterns of the input data, graphs can be drawn using matplotlib. As shown in 2 graphs, the sepal length and sepal width do not show a clear pattern and there are many overlaps between Virginica and Versicolor, so these data are ignored although the accuracy level will drop a little bit. Instead, the petal length and petal width are used as input data, which shows clear pattern.

The next step is to pre-process the data. The petal length and corresponding petal width are combined together to form a new (2*150) array which is then transposed to a (150 * 2) array using .T method, so that one line stands for the feature of one flower i.e. length and width and there are characteristics of 150 flowers inputted. To transpose an array means to swap the column and row of the array. By the way, in order to have an easier interpretation of 3 types of Iris Flowers, 0,1,2 are given to them respectively.

In addition, as usual, the dataset is divided into training set (80%) and test set (20%).

Then, in order to simplify the following model, the 0,1,2 are changed to one-hot encoding. This means: 1 –> [1,0,0] 2 –>[0,1,0] 3 –>[0,0,1] , all of which are matrix form. This can be done using built-in method of tensorflow.

To visualise the separated data sets and training sets, the matplotlib is used again. Note that, for example, train_x[train_y == 0,1] means that to find the element in the 1st column which has the index of train_y == 0. For instance, if x = np.array([a,b,c,d,e]), y = np.array([1,2,3,1,4]), when x[y==1] is inputted, the output should be array([a,d]).

After pre-processing the data, model is needed to be described and trained. Here, 3 layers are used. There are 2 inputs to the input layer, which are width and length respectively, and the output of which has 64 values. This is the input of the hidden layer, which gives 64 outputs to the output layer. Finally, the output layer gives 3 outputs (0,1,2), indicating 3 types of Iris flowers, and the values of which is called the confidence score. The higher the confidence score in one neuron, the higher the probability the type represented by the neuron will be. Furthermore, in the first two layers, the activation function ‘relu (rectified linear unit)’ is used, and in the output layer, the function ‘softmax’ is used. By the way, the Dense layers mean that every neurons are connected to each other.

model.summary() can be used to see the structure of the model. As shown in the table, there are overall 4547 parameters needed to be trained. Additionally, the loss function used is ‘mse (mean square error)’ and the optimizer used is ‘sgd (stochastic gradient descent)’, after which the model is trained for 100 times . Note that ‘batch_size=4’ means that 4 sets of data are trained together and ‘verbose=1’ , which is a default, means that the progress bar is printed. If verbose=0, the progress bar will not be printed.

The final step is to evaluate the trained model using the test sets of data.

We can see the predicted data, which has 3 columns, showing 3 confidence score. The highest score indicates the predicted results. For example, in the 0st row, 0.5292347is the highest among 3, so the predicted result is Setosa. Therefore, the index of the highest probability is taken out using the argmax() method in numpy.

Then, we can visualize the result by plotting graphs. The Ground Truth of the test sets is drawn, and the predicted value is drawn. As shown in the graph, most predictions are accurate, but there are still 2 inaccurate points shown. What is more, the predicted areas representing 3 types of Iris Flowers are drawn, so that the distribution of 3 types of flowers is more easily seen.

In conclusion, I have learnt and reviewed 4 things basically:

  1. Plot and select a suitable dataset
  2. Simplify the data before training
  3. Train the data by constructing a neural network
  4. Evaluate the model and show the final result

entry.title: Numpy 2
entry.content:
Nearest Ethnicity

(image from:https://image.shutterstock.com/image-photo/diversity-race-ethnicity-people-concept-260nw-527742886.jpg)

People around the world are from different ethnicities, so how close is it between different ethnicities?

Today, I wrote a code to find the nearest genes among 4, which means the nearest ethnicity group and the farthest one are found in the given 4 ethnicity groups.

The main idea here is to represent each type of genes in terms of vectors with 4 dimensions, each of which stands for the proportion of corresponding genes.

First of all, the 4 types of genes of four different ethnicities are inputted using numpy array. Also, 0,1,2,3 are used to represent Eskimos, Bantu, British and Korean respectively.

Then, the ‘vectors’ are unitized, which means to divide each component by the magnitude of the ‘vector’. Hence, I define a function called ‘unitize’ here. After this, the four unitized vectors are combined to form 1 array called ‘total’.

After unitizing the vector, the distance is calculated, and this is the core step. Also, I used the idea of ‘k nearest neighbour’ mentioned in the blog numpy. The 44 array is changed into a 4 * 1 4 array and a 1 * 4 * 4 array, and then the deduction is performed by using the broadcasting rules. Therefore, both arrays becomes a 4 * 4 * 4 array. During the change from 4 * 1* 4 to 4 * 4 * 4, it means that the types of genes (A1,A2,B,O) for each ethnicity are repeated 4 times, and during the change from 1 * 4* 4 to 4 * 4 * 4, it means that the information of 4 different ethnicities is repeated 4 times, so that the deduction is to calculate the ‘distance’ between corresponding genes.

Finally, the result is printed.

In conclusion, the main point here is:

  1. Use vector to represent the percentage of type of genes
  2. Use broadcasting to calculate the gene distance
  3. Figure out the nearest and the farthest ethnicities groups.

entry.title: Read & Write documents
entry.content:
Recently, I have learnt how to read and write documents in Python using absolute path (绝对路径) and relative path (相对路径).

Create IELTS vocabulary test

Given that I have got an IELTS vocabulary sources, I can randomly create several numbers of tests and corresponding answers with the same contents but different order of the question, so that the cheating can be avoided. The vocabulary source is shown below.

I open the file in the C drive and read the contents. Note that the ‘encoding = ‘UTF-8’’ is used because there are some Chinese in the documents. Otherwise, the Chinese cannot be recognised. The created documents are shown below.

Then, the ‘split()’ method is used to separate the English word and its Chinese meaning, which is followed by putting all the English into a list called ‘word’ and all the Chinese into a list called ‘chinese’. Here, I used a seemingly ‘stupid’ way to achieve this. As the list ‘content’ is like one Chinese, one word, I separate them according to their index (the word has odd one, and the Chinese has the even one), and after which I put the English word and corresponding Chinese into a dictionary, so that the Chinese meaning can be found more easily, which is not restricted by the index of the elements unlike the list. This is what line 1-17 did.

Then, I created 3 tests in the folder called ‘QA’ in the current working directory (当前工作目录) in the ‘write’ form. In the ‘Header’ part, the title of the tests are created, including name, date and the unique test number. Note that the ‘\n’ is used to achieve newline. Then, a list called ‘English’ is produced, with the same contents as the list ‘word’ but different corresponding index of the word, and this is done by the ‘random.shuffle()’

The next step is to create questions. By choosing an English word (keys) at a time and finding its corresponding Chinese meaning (values) in the dictionary, the word wanted to be test can be decided. Also, another 3 wrong options needs to be found, given that the multiple choice has 4 options. This can be done by deleting the correct answer in all the answers, and then randomly choose 3 of the new list. Finally, combine the correct answer with 3 incorrect ones, the options are finished.

The final step is to write the question and answers. The quiz form 1 is shown below.

In summary, 3 steps are required to deal with a document:

  1. Open the file using ‘open()’ method or create a file using ‘os.makedirs()’
  2. Use the ‘read()’ or ‘write()’ or ‘add()’ method to read or write or add something. Note that the ‘write()’ method will delete all the previous contents and write.
  3. Close the file using ‘close()’ method.

entry.title: Numpy
entry.content:
Recently, I have learnt something about the numpy, which is a module used to process array or matrix. The most useful and interesting one I found is the algorithm called the ‘k nearest neighbours’ , which is used to find the nearest 2 points of a given point.

This is used to to generate a 10*2 array, in which the values are in the range [0,1).

The random points are visualised below.

This code is used to find the distance between two points using the formula: ((x-x0)^2+(y-y0)^2)^0.5.

First of all, x[:,np.newaxis,:] represents a 10 * 1 * 2 array, where 10 stands for 10 points including itself, and 1*2 stands for the coordinate of the point, because (x,y) is a 1 * 2 array.

Then, x[np.newaxis,:,:] represents a 1 * 10 * 2 array. This stands for the coordinates of 10 points including the point itself.

In order to make it possible to make a deduction, the broadcasting is used here. Both of 2 arrays are broadcasted to a 10 * 10 * 2 array, meaning that each point undergoes a deduction with other 10 points including itself. This can be illustrated using the following diagram.

Consider the point x0:

Consider the point x1:

and so on

The total deduction is :

(10,10,2)

(10,10)

Here, the partition(x,n) is used to find the nth smallest value and replace these values to the front of the array, and the rest will be arranged randomly. For example, if the input is np.partition([1,3,6,2,5],2), the output will be [1,2,3,6,5]. Hence, the argpartition() is used to find the index of 2 smallest point around the given point. Note that (k+1) is used, because the nearest point to the given point is itself.

This programme is very useful in real life. For example, DiDi or Uber, which are Taxi apps. The system will find the nearest taxi around you, and inform the driver according to your location. How is this achieved? The main principle here is the ‘k nearest neighbours’. The system will calculate the relative distance between taxis and your position, and find the taxi with the shortest distance, so that the taxi can arrive as soon as possible.

(image from: https://www.iphonefaq.org/files/styles/large/public/uber_driver.jpg?itok=gIi7e_W2)

Also, in the social media, ‘People you might know’ will be recommended to you from time to time. How is this achieved? I believe the main principle here is also the ‘k nearest neighbours’. If you contact with the person who is also often contacted by that person, that person may have common friends with you. Therefore, if your social cycle is similar to another person, this indicates that you might know each other. In other words, if your ‘k nearest neighbours’ is similar to that of another person, then you may know each other.

(Image from: https://3e8srg4b6qwh33x5ic36aap8-wpengine.netdna-ssl.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/01/IG-STEP-13.jpg)

entry.title: Neural Network-based Regression
entry.content:
Recently, I have learnt the Neural Network-based Regression, which means that according to train set and test set of data, a prediction can be made according to the model based on neural network.

First of all, a csv. file of operation cost and corresponding profit is read to the notebook, and the cost and profit are separated.

Then, we can use matplotlib to plot the scattered graph to see the distribution of data, and after which we can separate the whole data into test data (20%)and train data (80%).

Due to the fact that the data is too big to deal with, we need to normalise the value by dividing all data by the their mean, so that the data becomes smaller, which is easier to handle and looks more clear.

The next step is to describe a neural network, which, in my opinion, is the most important and core step. We use the sequential() to construct the model, which is multiple layers packed together. Here, 3 layers are used, which are input layer, hidden layer and output layer respectively, and there is 1 input data, which is a tuple, consisting of cost and profit, followed by 16 output to the hidden layer, followed by 16 output to the output layer. Then, after calculation in the activation function, which is relu (Rectified Linear Unit) here, the result of which becomes the output.

The neural network looks like this:

By the way, the .summary()method can be used to print the brief description of the model.

The next step is to train the model, using either .train_on_batch() or .fit(), and I choose the latter because it operates faster. The loss function used is mse (Mean Square Error) and the optimizer used is SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent). Then. we can plot a graph to visualise the model being trained.

After training the model, we can evaluate the model in terms of cost. Furthermore, the weight of each neuron can be printed, which seems very complicated.

The final step is to change the processed data to the original data, i.e. denormalization.

The final graph looks like this:

After learning the Neural Network-based Regression, I have understood the importance of machine learning more deeply. We cannot write every code to achieve complex things, so the code must enable the programme to learn and find the common features of the data in order to predict what would happen. Just like this example, a CEO can determine its cost by predicting his/her expected profit. This increases the certainty for a company.

entry.title: The biggest failure I experienced
entry.content:
Being a successful student requires a variety of skills including time management and keeping good focus; I learnt this important fact during my experience of failing the ZhongKao exam which at the time, had a very negative impact on me. Ultimately though, it taught me a lot, and offered me insight into what failure can motivate us to achieve although it can be a very difficult learning curve.

<Image from Here>

During the 3 years of studying at Middle High school, I did not study very hard and I clearly remember that in the 2nd year, almost each afternoon, I could be found playing badminton after school.

<Image from Here )

After two academic years, I realized that my mock exam score had declined rapidly due to my focus on other activities, so in the 3rd year, which is the last year of Junior High, I decided to study harder, but it was too late to return to my previous high standards. Eventually, when I received my score for the very important test, I was shocked because due to my poor score, I couldn’t apply to any mainstream schools which was my dream. At that moment, I felt tearful and distraught but on reflection I realised I had to analyse why my performance was so poor and discover my underlying problem.

<Image from Here)

The subsequent soul-searching and quest to take something positive from my experience of failure led me to some important revelations. First of all, think twice before doing anything; If I had thought about the result of playing badminton instead of studying every day, I would have studied much harder. By studying harder for these 3 years, many years later I could then enjoy playing. In addition, due to this poor application to my studies and low motivation, I could have regretted it for many years in the future Therefore, nowadays, before doing anything, I always ask myself three questions: Would you regret it if you do not do it? Is it the best decision to make at the moment given my current circumstances? What are the advantages and disadvantages of not doing it?’. These questions now allow me try to make more rational decisions.

<Image from Here )

Secondly, I have learnt the importance of a character trait called persistence. I have come to realise that every success can rarely exist without persistence, although it can be argued that it is often easier to give up when life becomes challenging and look for a way out. Through my experience of failing and losing motivation, it is clear that temptations such as electronic devices and phone calls cause someone to get distracted and become unfocused, and if this happens, I will think about the difference between who I am currently and who I want to be in the future. Suddenly, I will refocus on the meaningful things, which include long-term goals and my own set of values which include patience and perseverance.

<Image from Here)

This new approach and mindset have resulted in excellent results in Senior High School. For example, after working hard for years, I have become the top student in the monthly test and the end of semester tests, and now I continue to work hard to maintain my focus and determination, hoping to apply to a good university.

<Image from Here )

My final piece of advice is just do it instead of thinking too much. In the past, I sometimes had a plan, but possible imagined difficulties would cause me to give up easily. Now, instead, I just do it, and adjust the plan during the process, which I found is the most efficient way to take me to a successful outcome although it is true decisions have to be made on a case by case basis. For instance, several months ago, I hesitated as to whether I should learn the Python language, because it seemed very complicated and a step too far for me but I decided to try it. Indeed, initially, I could not understand any lines, but I did not give up as I may have done before. Spending more time and energy on it, I have finally learnt some useful skills, and during the process learnt, I began to study the subject area in more depth and I have been deeply involved in a waste disposal programme which I am finding very interesting.

<Image from Here)

In conclusion, after experiencing the biggest failure of my life, I have spent some valuable time finding the reasons for it and applied the solutions to my current studies and life. These realisations and insights discussed above have improved not only my studies but also my life skills, offering me a much more positive and balanced outlook on how failure can be understood as a learning experience for self- improvement and be the springboard for success rather than a reason for self-pity and discouragement.

<Image from Here)

entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
Hello, everyone! This is my first blog.

Quick Start

Create a new post

More info: Writing

Run server

More info: Server

Generate static files

More info: Generating

Deploy to remote sites

More info: Deployment

entry.title: The feelings after attending the BPho
entry.content:
On November 15th, I attended the BPho Physics competition. Although the content of the test was a little bit hard, I learned so much from the exam itself and the process of preparing for it.

At the beginning, facing so many difficult questions, I didn’t know what to do. As I was an AS level student, some of the questions are the ones that I hadn’t met before. As a result, after solving some questions, I decided to separate all the questions from 2008 to 2018 into 5 categories in Section 1 and 8 categories in Section 2.


Also, I made some comments, which is about some of my thinking process, on the side of the question.

After taking part in this exam, I have learned 3 good habits:

  • Being persistent. During the learning, not everything can be achieved without any obstacles. Although, once I wanted to give up, I kept telling myself ‘try again, try again’. Luckily, I did it, and regardless of the outcome, this spirit inspired me a lot.

  • Being confident. At the beginning, I was not confident at all, because I can solve few problems. As time went by, I began to make some progress, so I became more confident. Latter, I found that the more confident you are, the better the outcome will be, vice versa.

  • Being ambitious. Before doing a task, a target should be built up, which guided me when I was hesitated. Setting up a higher goal gives me many incentives to learn.

    What is more, after doing a lot of questions, I found that Physics was quite interesting. Many things in our daily lives can be explained by Physics. For example, one day, on my way to school, a car passed by with its trumpet on. The pitch of which changed as the car moving. A week later, I knew this is called the Doppler Effect.

Physics is very interesting!

entry.title: Visualising COVID-19 cases
entry.content:
Today, I have done some analysis on cases of COVID-19.

Total number of Confirmed & Deaths

The data was downloaded from ‘kaggle’ dataset.(https://www.kaggle.com/imdevskp/corona-virus-report)

It looks like this:

Among thousands of data, I was interested in cases in China, so I select the data from China.

There is still too much data, but they are all Chinese cases, so I decide to merge the data from the same province using ‘groupby()’ method, and then to find the total number of confirmed, deaths and recovered using ‘sum()’ method.

Then, I try to plot a bar chart to see the relative number.

As shown in the graph, confirmed cases in Hubei Province is significantly greater than that in other areas, because Wuhan, the capital city of Hubei, is the center of the outbreak of this pandemic. However, confirmed cases in other areas cannot be clearly seen and compared, so I decided to plot another graph excluding Hubei province.

As shown in this graph, Guangdong province has the second largest number of confirmed cases, maybe due to the fact that it is a international city that has a great number of flow of people around the world every day, which increses the spread of the disease.

Furthermore, I also drew the number of Deaths untill May 8th. As shown in the graph, although Guangdong has the second largest number of confirmed cases, the deaths in Guangdong province is not the second greatest. Instead, Henan has the highest number of deaths apart from Hubei, maybe because medical facilities are not enough in Henan, and doctors are not qualified as in Guangdong, meaning that the ability for Henan to deal with this COVID-19 is weaker.

Trend from Jan 22nd to May 8th

I am a person born in Jiangsu province, so I am particularly interested in data in Jiangsu, and I chose cities around Jiangsu to analyse the trend.

Here, the pivot table is used to arrange the data.

As shown in the graph, the overal trend for 4 cities is the same, which is increasing rapidly at first, and tends to reach a plateau. Also, Jiangsu is the city that reaches a plateau at first among 4 cities, which means the Jiangsu government has done a great job reducing the spread out of this disease.

The confirmed cases in many places in China tends to be constant, which means that as temperature increasing, the virus seems to be less and less active. Hopefully, the coronavirus will dissapear as soon as possible.

entry.title: Pandas 2
entry.content:
Today, I learnt some functions and applications of the modules Pandas through the data about the number of bicycle passing through the Fremon Bridge in Seattle in the US.

<Image from https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/4e/Fremont_Bridge.jpg>

First of all, the data is pre-processed by simplifying the names and drop the NaN data.

Here, the .eval() method is just like data['Total'] = data['West'] + data['East'] .

To visualise the data:

As shown in the graph, the data is too much and only some of the data is needed to be analysed, so the resampling is necessary.

Here, the mean value of 30 days are chosen to be the size of a window which rolls across all the data, and the central value is used to be the label.

In comparison, if 50 days are chosen to be the size of a window, the curve will look more smooth, which will be similar to the shape of a sinusoidal wave.

If we want to see which time period has the greatest number of bicycles passing by, data for the same period of time can be grouped by.

The data.index.dayofweek gives values of 0-6, each of which is repeated for 24 times because there are 24 hours a day, and the data is collected each hour. Then, 0-6 are given names of Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday……

Finally, the number of bicycles in weekday and weekend can be shown.

np.where(a(bool),b,c) means that if a is True, b is returned, while a is False, c is returned. Therefore, for the first 5 days(0,1,2,3,4), data.index.weekday < 5 is True, it is weekday, and for the remaining 2 days, data.index.weekday < 5 is False, it is weekend.

entry.title: Pandas
entry.content:
Recently, I have learnt some interesting functions and methods about the module Pandas, which are often used to process tables.

<Image from https://i.ytimg.com/vi/ZxC771f04rQ/maxresdefault.jpg>

I saw an interesting data yesterday which was about the history of the Google stock price.

After downloading the data from the pandas_datareader, I saw the head (first 5 lines) of the stock price from 2004-08-19.

Also, matplotlib can be used to visualize the closed price here. As shown in the graph, although there were some fluctuations around 2008, the overall trend of the stock price is rising.

Then, I have learnt how to resample the data according to new frequency, such as ‘D’ for day, and ‘H’ for hour. Here, the ‘BA’ stands for business year end, so the mean price of each year is calculated, and the value of which is put on the last working day of the year. This is shown by the dotted dashed line. By contrast, asfreq('BA') is used to find the price of the last working day of the year, so the curve is less smooth than the resampled one.

What is more, I also learnt the shift() and tshift() method. shift() method is used to shift the data forward or backward and tshift() is used to shift the DateTimeIndex. Here, in the first graph, it is the original data, and in the 2nd graph, the data shifts to the right by 900 days, so there is NaN data on the left. In the 3rd graph, the DateTimeIndex shifts to the left by 900 days. The red vertical line is drawn to represent the same position on the curve. By the way, loc=2 in the legend method is used to locate the legend in the top left corner, and .set(weight='heavy') is used to make the font bold.

In addition, the shift in time can be applied to see the return of investing.

If the data shifts to the left by 365 days i.e. 1 year, and divides by the original data and minus 1, this will be the percentage change in the value of the stock. For example, if the price in 2006 is $100, and the price in 2007 is $120, the percentage increase in value is 2007/2006-1 .

Finally, I learnt the rolling method of the DataFrame and Series, which was used to achieve the rolling view.

The main idea here, in my opinion, is to choose 365 data, and to set the centre value as the label, meaning that the value of either .mean() or .std() or something else is given to the central value to represent the whole 365 data. This reminds me of the convolution operation which I learnt in the Garbage Classification programme. The rolling view is quite similar to the convolution in the CNN (Convolutional Neural Network). The convolutional kernel here is 365 * 1 matrix, and each 365 data undergoes the convolution.

Just like the graph that I have sketched here, 365 days are a window, and the window roll across the data, which is similar to the idea of convolution.

In conclusion, I have learnt:

  1. DateTimeIndex
  2. Resample & asfreq() methods
  3. ‘ffill()’ & ‘bfill()’ in asqfreq() methods
  4. shift() & tshift() methods
  5. Rolling View (analogous to CNN)

entry.title: Strings
entry.content:
Yesterday, I have learnt more methods about how to deal with strings.

Create a password

First of all, I wrote a code to help the users to create a password which satisfies the requirements:

  1. The length of the password MUST be greater than 8

  2. The password MUST be a combination of letters & numbers. Either there are only letters or there are only numbers will NOT be allowed.

  3. There must be a Capital letter involved.
    The codes are posted below:

    The flow chart is shown below.

    At the beginning, the ‘while’ loop is used, because the number of times the users will try to create the password is unknown. Then, the ‘isalpha()’ , ‘isdecimal()’ and ‘isalnum()’ methods are used.

    isalpha(): return True if the string only contains letters

    isdecimal(): return True if the string only contains numbers

    isalnum(): return True if the string only contains numbers & letters

What is more, to decide whether a Capital letter is involved in the password, the lower() method is used. This helps to change all the letters in the string into the lowercase. If there is a Capital letter involved, the changed string will be different from the original string, while if there is no Capital letters involved, the alterred string will be exactly the same as before. Note that the numbers will NOT change if the lower() method is used.

Replacing word

This programme helps users to find the content and replace it with another.

  1. Split the string into each word
  2. Find the word and replace it
  3. Join the list into compelete sentences

The main idea is listed above, and there is an example below, which aims to replace the pronoun ‘which’ with ‘that’.
